title: Girls Night

jokes.text: He seemed quite taken with an older woman, so I thought best not to intrude.
jokes.setup: Moira is relaying a benign-seeming observation about Ted at the café.
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of 'an older woman' is dropped as if it's not a grenade — the audience knows this is devastating news for Alexis before Alexis processes it.
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira obliviousness as a delivery mechanism. The joke works on two levels: her blithe delivery and the audience's anticipation of Alexis's reaction. Rewatch bonus because you clock Moira's cluelessness even harder the second time.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What did Ted say when you saw him?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: He was with an older woman.

jokes.text: Love that for him.
jokes.setup: Moira says Ted sends his regards and was 'quite taken with an older woman.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's performatively unbothered 'Love that for him' is a paper-thin façade — the audience immediately recognizes the forced positivity masking hurt.
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfectly Alexis. The phrase became a cultural touchstone from this show. Supreme economy — the joke is entirely in the gap between what she says and what she means. High quotability because it's entered common usage.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if she were his mother, the canoodling would be significantly more concerning.

jokes.text: They were canoodling in the banquette for all to see, so I'd be rather concerned if that were his mother, David.
jokes.setup: David tries to soften the blow by suggesting the woman could be a mother or close friend.
jokes.punchline: Moira's deadpan logical takedown — 'rather concerned if that were his mother' — obliterates David's rescue attempt with Moirish precision.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure — David's attempt at comfort is the setup, and Moira's response is both logically sound and devastatingly delivered. 'Canoodling in the banquette' is peak Moira vocabulary. The word 'canoodling' alone earns half the laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was more of a... twisted rope situation happening on the side of her head.

jokes.text: Okay. Um, in other news, I recently discovered that I'm allergic to pitted fruits.
jokes.setup: A tense emotional moment as the Ted revelation lands on Alexis.
jokes.punchline: David's non-sequitur attempt to change the subject is simultaneously self-serving and weirdly specific — 'pitted fruits' is absurdly mundane as an escape hatch.
jokes.explanation: Pure David Rose: self-absorbed, socially inept, but somehow endearing. The specificity of 'pitted fruits' is funnier than a generic deflection. Strong character integration — this is exactly what David would do.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going for a jog.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: In that?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? It's fine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're wearing a full face of makeup and what appears to be a silk blouse.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So?

jokes.text: Canoodling? — reaction beat from Alexis as the word hits her.
jokes.setup: David's awkward topic-change overlaps with Alexis finally processing Moira's word choice.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's one-word echo of 'Canoodling?' signals the dam breaking — the audience laughs at her delayed horrified realization amid David's oblivious babbling.
jokes.explanation: The comic timing is the joke — Alexis's one-word reaction cuts through David's rambling. Works because it's a perfectly placed beat that confirms her true feelings despite her earlier breezy dismissal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you were wrong. You were wrong about Ted, you were wrong about Heather, and frankly, you were wrong about being right about being wrong.

jokes.text: No, no braid. Uh, looked more like thick, healthy hair woven together loosely.
jokes.setup: Alexis desperately asks if the woman had a braid — trying to identify whether it's Heather.
jokes.punchline: Moira's painstaking description of a braid without using the word braid, delivered with total sincerity, is absurdly unhelpful and perfectly Moira.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Moira describes a braid in elaborate terms while clearly not knowing what a braid is called, and her sincerity makes it land harder. The specificity of 'thick, healthy hair woven together loosely' is both absurd and exactly how someone with Moira's self-image would perceive hair. Strong originality — a braid description as comedic set piece is unexpected.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, she's charming to everyone who isn't Alexis.

jokes.text: I can't believe he's still dating Heather. / I know, it's crazy. / I believe that was her name, Heather.
jokes.setup: Alexis names 'Heather'; David agrees; then Moira casually confirms 'I believe that was her name, Heather' as if she'd known all along.
jokes.punchline: Moira's belated confirmation that she actually knew the name the whole time makes her entire vague description retroactively more absurd.
jokes.explanation: A tight callback that recontextualizes the previous joke. Moira knew the name all along, which makes her labored hair description funnier in retrospect. Three-character rhythm — Alexis devastated, David sympathetic, Moira oblivious — is perfectly orchestrated.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I thought it was a compliment. She has a very striking face. Like a trout that's been struck by lightning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, that's not better.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, what do you want me to say? That she's beautiful? She's not beautiful, David. She's striking. There's a difference.

jokes.text: Ok, um, but you're in like a day dress. / Mhmm, I am.
jokes.setup: Alexis announces she's going for a jog to escape the conversation about Ted.
jokes.punchline: David points out she's in a day dress; Alexis cheerfully confirms this and jogs off anyway, the lie completely transparent.
jokes.explanation: A quick visual-comedy beat — the image of Alexis in a day dress 'jogging' to avoid emotional conversation is funny in context. Not sophisticated but efficiently executed. The 'Mhmm, I am' confirmation is perfectly Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, we're wasting a lot of valuable space in this office. It's just sitting here, empty.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, what did you have in mind?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I already installed a beer fridge back there.

jokes.text: Alexis announcing 'I am going for a jog' while wearing a day dress
jokes.setup: Alexis wants to escape the painful conversation; David notes 'you're in like a day dress'
jokes.punchline: She confirms: 'Mhmm, I am.' — fully committing to the jog in inappropriate clothing as her exit strategy
jokes.explanation: The visual of Alexis sprinting off in a day dress is inherently funny, and her unapologetic confirmation ('Mhmm, I am') is a perfect deadpan beat. Character-specific — Alexis's vanity and her emotional avoidance colliding in one image.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: How does bringing the fridge help me?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, your wife wanted it out of the house, and I wanted it in mine, so really, I'm helping both of you.

jokes.text: Okay, I have never heard someone say so many wrong things, one after the other, consecutively, in a row.
jokes.setup: Moira has just delivered a series of devastatingly insensitive comments about Ted and Alexis.
jokes.punchline: David's indignant complaint is itself a comedic redundancy — 'one after the other, consecutively, in a row' is deliberately repetitive, a verbal tic that shows his own fluster.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the structure of the sentence itself — 'one after the other, consecutively, in a row' is three ways of saying the same thing, which is funny both as a character reveal (David's flustered verbosity) and as a self-aware writing choice. Good quotability because it's usable in real life.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, I sold a case of beer to one of my staff members today.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... something.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Actually, I bought them myself. Haven't even drunk them yet.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So you sold yourself beer?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I like to support local business.

jokes.text: A year later, and she is like right back in it.
jokes.setup: Moira defends herself; David pivots to addressing the actual situation.
jokes.punchline: David's dry summary of Alexis's emotional state — delivered to Moira as a scolding — is both accurate and funny because he's also subtly covering for his sister.
jokes.explanation: Functional line that reveals character — David is more emotionally attuned than he admits. The laugh is mild; it's more of a grounding beat than a punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Where would the TV go?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you could put it on that wall there, or that wall there, or really any of these walls.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Right, but where would YOU put it?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, I'd probably put it on one of the walls.

jokes.text: Oh. I... I did call that other woman charming, didn't I?
jokes.setup: David has just chided Moira for being oblivious to Alexis's feelings.
jokes.punchline: Moira's dawning self-awareness that she called Ted's girlfriend 'striking' (she now misremembers it as 'charming') is a perfect beat — the self-recrimination is still slightly wrong.
jokes.explanation: The callback is layered: she realizes she said something awful, but she remembers the wrong compliment ('charming' vs 'striking'). This is consistent with Moira's grandiosity — even in guilt, she's not quite accurate. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Did you know that bears can curse? I read it somewhere. Well, not actual cursing, but they make these vocalizations that sound remarkably like profanity when you really listen.

jokes.text: Striking, I believe was the word you used to describe Ted's new girlfriend, who isn't Alexis.
jokes.setup: Moira misremembers the word she used.
jokes.punchline: David's correction — with the devastating appendage 'who isn't Alexis' — is perfectly calibrated cruelty-with-love.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: The joke works on three levels simultaneously — it's a correction, a callback, and a devastating emotional gut-punch all in one clause. 'Who isn't Alexis' does enormous work with minimal words. The economy is exceptional. It's written with exactly the precision you'd expect from the best version of this show.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yes, there was a group text, but we didn't include you.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We figured you'd just complain about the group dynamics anyway.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would not.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You literally complain about everything.

jokes.text: Well to my credit, she was.
jokes.setup: David has just reminded Moira she called Ted's new girlfriend 'striking.'
jokes.punchline: Moira's unrepentant confirmation — 'to my credit, she was' — is the perfect capstone: she understands she caused harm but doubles down on the accuracy of her assessment.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.6 craft / 9.0 impact: This is the episode's single funniest line in the cold open. Five words that encapsulate Moira's entire character: self-awareness, total lack of guilt, aesthetic standards above all else, and absurd logic. 'To my credit' as a preface to an unapologetic confirmation of an insult is a masterclass in character comedy. The scene earns this punchline across two full minutes of setup. It would get a sustained laugh from any comedy-savvy audience.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are we hanging out before work?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We're quite late.

jokes.text: There's a lotta wasted space in here. I mean this should be an area for guests to come and mix and mingle.
jokes.setup: Roland announces he wants to be a 'team player' and has a big idea for the motel.
jokes.punchline: His grand vision is to turn Johnny's office into a lounge — the self-serving logic (he wants a hangout spot) is dressed up as business improvement.
jokes.explanation: Competent Roland character comedy — his logic is transparently self-interested while being delivered with total conviction. Sets up the escalating bit well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because the lip balms have been in that spot for two years, Patrick. Two years. People know where they are. It's muscle memory. You move them, and suddenly someone reaches for a lip balm in the dark and they get a handful of mints instead. What if they're driving? What if they crash? What if they die?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What if it's not just a crash, Patrick? What if someone sues us? What if we lose the store? What if we end up living in a car, and I'm forced to apply my skincare routine at a rest stop using the bathroom mirror under those horrible fluorescent lights?

jokes.text: That's what their rooms are for.
jokes.setup: Roland suggests guests should be able to 'mix and mingle' in Johnny's office.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's literal and slightly horrified response reveals his protective relationship with his workspace.
jokes.explanation: Short, clean, in character. The logic is slightly off (guests mixing in rooms is also weird) which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't just move them. They've been arranged in a very specific order, and if I move them without consulting them first, I'm essentially uprooting their entire lives.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They have feelings, you know. They have families. Some of them have been together for years.

jokes.text: Beer fridge reveal — Roland has installed a full beer fridge in Johnny's office.
jokes.setup: Roland has been building to a big reveal about his idea for the space.
jokes.punchline: The reveal is a beer fridge — the escalation from 'lounge area' to actual appliance installation shows Roland has already acted unilaterally.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation beat — the comedy is in Roland having already installed it, not asking permission. The pause before the reveal ('but do the rooms have a...') is well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You can't just move things around the store without consulting me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not moving things around arbitrarily, Patrick. That lamp was fugly and it was throwing off the entire feng shui of the space. The lighting was too harsh, the placement was uninspired, and quite frankly, it looked like something you'd find in a hotel room designed by someone who's never seen a hotel room.

jokes.text: Joce wanted a little extra space for the new baby, and I thought why not help out a buddy in the process?
jokes.setup: Johnny asks where the beer fridge came from.
jokes.punchline: Roland frames dumping his fridge at work as an act of generosity toward Johnny — 'help out a buddy' — completely inverting the reality.
jokes.explanation: Roland's self-serving reframing is consistent character work. The 'help out a buddy' line is the punchline — he genuinely believes this narrative.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, I remember that time you compromised.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Which one? There were so many.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I have no idea. I can't think of any.

jokes.text: Well, you can sell beers to the guests, Johnny, unless of course you're trying not to make money, which would explain a couple of things here.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks how a beer fridge helps him.
jokes.punchline: Roland's backhanded suggestion that Johnny is secretly trying not to make money — 'which would explain a couple of things here' — is an insult delivered as business advice.
jokes.explanation: Good Roland line — the insulting subtext ('your motel is failing') delivered as helpful advice is a nice character moment. The qualifier 'which would explain a couple of things' is the funniest part.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I compromised. I let you pick the movie.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You let me pick between two movies you wanted to see.

jokes.text: Would that staff member be you? / Yes, it would. I'm gonna have 'em after lunch.
jokes.setup: Roland proudly announces he's already sold 2 beers to a staff member.
jokes.punchline: The staff member is Roland himself, and he's already planned when to drink them.
jokes.explanation: Tight two-beat joke. The setup is perfectly constructed — 'already sold 2 beers to a staff member' sounds promising, then the self-dealing reveal lands cleanly. 'I'm gonna have 'em after lunch' is a great button that shows Roland's full commitment to the bit.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I have a system. I go through the reviews, I read the synopsis, I check the ratings...

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, that's not a compromise. That's you doing exactly what you want and then explaining it to me afterward.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, what would you suggest?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: A compromise is when we both give up something we want. So maybe I pick a movie, and you pick a movie, and we watch one of those movies.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: ...That's still just picking a movie.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yes, but only one of us gets their way, which is the definition of compromise.

jokes.text: Now the big question is where to put the TV?
jokes.setup: Johnny has barely processed the beer fridge.
jokes.punchline: Roland casually reveals there's also a TV coming — the escalation from 'lounge area' to fridge to TV shows his plan is already fully formed and unstoppable.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — the 'big question is where to put the TV' assumes the TV is already decided. Roland's oblivious momentum is funny and consistent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm just going to run out and get some tote bags.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And I'm sure that the second I leave, you're all going to sit around and discuss my cryotherapy.

jokes.text: Well, Johnny, you can't expect guests to sit around with their beers staring at you.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks 'What TV?' as if refusing to accept this reality.
jokes.punchline: Roland's explanation — that guests can't just stare at Johnny — is both absurd (the beer fridge was also Roland's idea) and weirdly logical.
jokes.explanation: Good line — Roland's concern about the guest experience is entirely self-created. 'Staring at you' is a funny specific detail that implies Johnny himself is the least entertaining part of the bar area.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You guys are gonna talk about me after I leave, right?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh, absolutely.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yeah, immediately.

jokes.text: I cursed those bears with the little strength that I could muster up.
jokes.setup: David arrives at the store to find Patrick and Stevie hanging out before work.
jokes.punchline: Patrick is mid-sentence on what is clearly an absurd story — we catch only the tail end, which is somehow funnier for being decontextualized.
jokes.explanation: An excellent cold-open-style joke for the scene — we hear only the most absurd clause of what must be a wild story. The humor is in NOT explaining. 'The little strength I could muster up' implies a harrowing encounter that we'll never hear about. High quotability out of context.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's wonderful that Alexis is finally becoming a philanthropist. A real friend of the Phil.

jokes.text: But yes, there is a chain, and no, you're not on it.
jokes.setup: David asks if there was a text chain he wasn't included on, clearly expecting 'no, of course not.'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan confirmation that yes, the chain exists and David is excluded is funnier than a denial.
jokes.explanation: A perfect Stevie line — dry, slightly mean, economical. The structure is excellent: the question sets up an expected denial, and Stevie's blunt confirmation is the subversion. High character integration because only Stevie delivers this with this particular flatness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Alexis is more of a... pop-up book. You know, the kind where you open it and things just... fly out at you unexpectedly, and half of them are broken.

jokes.text: ...so for all we know, they could be poison, and we're moving the lip balms, best sellers, all the way to the corner, here.
jokes.setup: David objects to new breath mints being placed near the cash register.
jokes.punchline: David's leap from 'new product' to 'could be poison' is an absurd escalation that perfectly captures his dramatic risk-aversion.
jokes.explanation: Good David moment — his catastrophizing is played completely straight, which makes it funnier. The 'could be poison' line is peak David risk assessment.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I wasn't gossiping, I was just making conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Twyla, gossip is just conversation with a price tag on it.

jokes.text: They're very delicious.
jokes.setup: David worries the new mints could be poison.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, immediate 'They're very delicious' — she's already eaten them — deflates David's dramatic concern in the most casual way possible.
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character integration. Stevie's three-word dismissal is both factual and deeply unhelpful to David's argument. The casualness — she's just been eating the mints this whole time — is the funniest part.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not Erin Brockovich. That's just my wife in a wig.

jokes.text: David you move things without consulting me all the time. Like the brooms! / Okay, well they were fugly brooms with big red handles. They didn't match our sand and stone colour palette.
jokes.setup: Patrick catches David in a double standard — David moves things constantly without consulting Patrick.
jokes.punchline: David's defense of the broom relocation ('fugly brooms,' 'sand and stone colour palette') is delivered with total seriousness — the specificity makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: The word 'fugly' is funny in David's mouth, and 'sand and stone colour palette' as a justification for broom relocation is absurdly specific and pitch-perfect David Rose aesthetics comedy. Patrick's delivery of the catch is clean.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you're not funny.

jokes.text: Nothing, I just um, just remembering all those times that you compromised. I was just thinking about the same things, because there, there's so many to flip through.
jokes.setup: David insists he compromises all the time.
jokes.punchline: Stevie and Patrick exchanging a silent look and then Stevie's barely-suppressed 'remembering all those times you compromised' — the pause and the 'so many to flip through' from Patrick is a beautifully coordinated two-person deadpan.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly executed two-hander. 'So many to flip through' implies an embarrassingly short mental archive. The relay between Stevie and Patrick shows they've bonded over David and are now a unit against him, which is itself a relationship-comedy payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So you two are brothers?

dialogue.speaker: Guest 1
dialogue.line: Yes, we are.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Right, right. And you get along? I mean, there's no weird tension or anything? Because I had a brother once, well, I still have a brother, but we don't really talk anymore. It's complicated. Anyway, do you guys ever fight? Like, real fights or just the normal sibling stuff?

jokes.text: You made me pick between two Sandra Bullock vehicles. / And you picked 'The Lake House', which was the correct choice.
jokes.setup: David claims letting Patrick pick the movie was a compromise.
jokes.punchline: Patrick reveals David only offered two Sandra Bullock films, and David's confident 'The Lake House was the correct choice' is peak David cultural taste.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.0 craft: This is exceptional character comedy. 'Two Sandra Bullock vehicles' is already funny; 'The Lake House was the correct choice' delivered with complete conviction makes it a standout. It reveals David's aesthetic hierarchy (The Lake House over Speed? Over Miss Congeniality?) in a way that's specific, surprising, and completely believable for him. High quotability because it's usable context-free.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I moved the plungers because David has this thing where he refuses to acknowledge that they exist. He acts like if he doesn't look at them, they're not real. So I put them in a cabinet where he wouldn't have to see them.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not true. I simply prefer to maintain a certain aesthetic in my home.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You told me last week that plungers are 'visual reminders of humanity's failures.'

jokes.text: Just so that you know, making someone choose between two things that you like, is not exactly a compromise.
jokes.setup: David insists The Lake House choice counts as a compromise.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's precise logical definition of what doesn't constitute a compromise — stated flatly — is both correct and devastating.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's role as the logical corrective to both David and Patrick is well-used here. The observation is accurate and relatable (audiences will recognize this dynamic from their own lives), which boosts impact.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose we could take the car, though I'm not sure of the current regulations on that sort of thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is drunk driving still frowned upon, or has that changed?

jokes.text: Fine. / Stevie, would you care to join me? / No. / Fair enough. So you guys are just gonna stay behind and talk about me after I've left. You know, swap stories about how I don't compromise.
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests David go pick up tote bags as a compromise.
jokes.punchline: David's accurate prediction of what Patrick and Stevie will do — said as a complaint — is itself evidence of his self-awareness despite his lack of change.
jokes.explanation: The joke is David's self-aware complaint — he knows exactly what's happening and resents it while being unable to change. 'Swap stories about how I don't compromise' is a good line because it's completely accurate and delivered as an accusation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The key to seduction is mystery and intrigue. You must make them wonder what you're thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I always start by locking eyes, then I slowly remove one glove, finger by finger, while maintaining an enigmatic smile.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Then I whisper something incomprehensible in a vaguely European accent. Works every time.

jokes.text: Apparently I've been taking too much time for myself, and I need to get back out there and meet people.
jokes.setup: Twyla explains why Alexis has organized a girls' night.
jokes.punchline: Twyla relaying Alexis's stated reason — which is actually Alexis's own emotional deflection about Ted — as if it's straightforward advice is quietly funny given what we know.
jokes.explanation: Functional plot-comedy beat — the irony is clear to the audience (Alexis needs the outing more than Twyla) but it's not a sharp joke, more of a dramatic irony setup.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm gonna pass on that, Mom.

jokes.text: Always the little philanthrope.
jokes.setup: Moira hears about Alexis organizing the girls' night for Twyla.
jokes.punchline: 'Philanthrope' — not quite 'philanthropist' — is a Moira-ism: a word that may or may not exist, used with total confidence. The irony is that Alexis is doing it for herself.
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira vocabulary joke — 'philanthrope' sounds like a word she invented or misremembered. The irony (Alexis is actually organizing this for herself) layered beneath Moira's oblivious assessment makes it richer. Very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was at this party, and John Cougar Mellencamp was there. I spent the entire evening positioning myself strategically. I wore my most alluring ensemble, I made myself available for conversation...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So did you go home with him?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I went home with your father.

jokes.text: Moira calls Heather 'Harriet' — getting the name wrong despite having established it earlier
jokes.setup: Moira is discussing Ted's girlfriend by name
jokes.punchline: She calls her 'Harriet,' having established the name 'Heather' earlier in the episode — Moira has already forgotten
jokes.explanation: A nice running gag — Moira cannot retain the name 'Heather.' The callback to the earlier scene rewards attentive viewers. The rewatch bonus is real because you spot it faster the second time.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My last target was John Cougar Mellencamp.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, what?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh yes. I perfected the technique on him backstage at Farm Aid.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that's... that's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, he certainly seemed affected.

jokes.text: Always the closed book, our Alexis. Bless her soul. / I've always found her to be pretty open about things. / A closed book that falls open the second you take it off the shelf.
jokes.setup: Moira describes Alexis as a 'closed book'; Twyla gently disagrees.
jokes.punchline: Moira's recovery — 'a closed book that falls open the second you take it off the shelf' — is a beautiful piece of Moira logic: she maintains her poetic framing while conceding Twyla's point.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.3 craft: This is an exceptional Moira line. It works as: (1) an elegant turn of phrase, (2) a perfect character reveal (Moira refuses to be wrong, only to be more precise), (3) an accurate description of Alexis, and (4) a joke that would be quoted out of context. 'A closed book that falls open the second you take it off the shelf' is the kind of line that ends up on quote cards. Very high quotability and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait! I can't leave the car looking like this. Does anyone have a compact mirror and a priest?

jokes.text: That's enough gossip for today, Twyla. No, I'm not comfortable discussing the intimate details of Alexis' private life, with her not present. / Oh, I wasn't gossiping, it was just that you brought it up.
jokes.setup: Moira has been pumping Twyla for information about Alexis and Ted.
jokes.punchline: Moira abruptly shuts down 'gossip' — when she was the instigator — and Twyla's confused, gentle reminder that Moira brought it up is perfectly delivered.
jokes.explanation: Good character hypocrisy joke. Moira's sudden performance of moral rectitude after being the gossip instigator is consistent and funny. Twyla's gentle factual correction ('you brought it up') lands because she's too sweet to be accusatory.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Roland, you need to stay at work. When you're home, you hover over me like I'm about to explode at any second. You follow me from room to room. You've installed a pregnancy pillow in every room of the house. You've started referring to yourself as 'we' when you talk about the baby. And yesterday, you made me a maternity gown out of a bed sheet because you said my regular clothes were 'too restrictive for the miracle.'

jokes.text: Gossip is the devil's telephone. Best to just hang up.
jokes.setup: Twyla points out Moira was the gossip initiator.
jokes.punchline: Moira ignores the accusation and delivers a dramatic aphorism — 'Gossip is the devil's telephone. Best to just hang up.' — as if she's on the moral high ground.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.3 craft: 'Gossip is the devil's telephone. Best to just hang up.' is a highly quotable Moira-ism. The aphorism is original, well-constructed, and completely hypocritical given that she was gossiping seconds ago. The telephone metaphor is unexpected but perfect. It's the kind of line that gets put on merchandise. Extremely high quotability — probably the most quoted line of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, okay... I see what you're saying, but did you SEE that move?! That was INSANE! Get him! GET HIM!

jokes.text: Well, Erin Brockovich has just started her job at the law firm, and she's not getting the respect she deserves, because of the way she dresses.
jokes.setup: Johnny walks in to find Roland has installed a TV and is showing guests a movie.
jokes.punchline: Roland's earnest plot summary of Erin Brockovich — as if Johnny asked — is both an absurd non-answer to the obvious question ('why is there a TV here?') and reveals Roland is genuinely invested in the film.
jokes.explanation: Good Roland moment — his complete obliviousness to what Johnny is actually asking, combined with genuine investment in Erin Brockovich's professional struggles, is funny. The plot summary as deflection is a solid character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Johnny, could you maybe keep Roland at the store a little bit longer today?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why? Is everything okay?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Yes, everything's fine. I just need him to stay longer. Please keep him longer. Just keep him there. As long as possible. Please.

jokes.text: Turns out, Carmine, here, is a big Albert Finney fan.
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to understand why there are guests in his office watching a movie.
jokes.punchline: Roland has not only installed a TV and invited guests but has learned their film preferences — the level of hospitality he's extended in Johnny's office, in Johnny's absence, is staggering.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Roland situation — he's become the de facto concierge of a makeshift lounge in one morning. The specificity of 'Albert Finney fan' is funnier than a generic movie preference.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You were right, Moira. I took your dating advice and it actually worked!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course it worked, darling. I'm a seasoned professional in matters of romance.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, it worked on the third try.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The third try?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Yeah, I had to message three different men before one of them responded.

jokes.text: Johnny, I thought you were joking. / Why would you think I was joking? / I dunno, maybe it's your dry wit, you know, that never quite hits the target.
jokes.setup: Johnny confronts Roland about the TV he specifically forbade.
jokes.punchline: Roland's explanation — that Johnny's dry wit 'never quite hits the target' — is both an insult to Johnny's communication skills and a defense of Roland's own obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Strong Roland line — his insult of Johnny's wit is delivered as an explanation, not an attack. 'Never quite hits the target' is a funny specific detail that implies Johnny thinks he's funnier than he is.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Hi, you've reached Ted Mullens. I'm either on the other line or away from my desk. Please leave a message and I'll get back to you as soon as possible. Oh, and if you heard a woman's voice just now saying 'Hi, you've reached...' that was just me doing a fake-out. I do that sometimes to keep people on their toes. Anyway, please leave a message.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Better than expected, huh?' — assuming Johnny is pleased with the TV setup
jokes.setup: Johnny has called Roland aside after discovering the movie screening
jokes.punchline: Roland's first assumption is that Johnny pulled him aside to compliment the operation
jokes.explanation: Roland's complete inability to read a room — assuming criticism is praise — is consistent character work. Brief, economical, and gets the laugh cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not good at compromise. It's like asking Beyoncé to just be okay with being in Destiny's Child forever. At a certain point, you have to accept that some people are meant to stand alone. I once told Patrick that I needed him to stop breathing so heavily when he sleeps, and he said, 'That's not a compromise, that's just controlling,' and I thought, 'Well, yes, but have you considered that I'm right?'

jokes.text: Roland to the guests: 'Keep it on pause, boys.' — treating a management reprimand as an intermission
jokes.setup: Johnny asks for a private word; Roland agrees to step aside
jokes.punchline: Roland's instruction to pause the movie — as if this is a perfectly normal interruption to a legitimate activity — is functionally absurd
jokes.explanation: Short but efficient — 'Keep it on pause, boys' accomplishes a lot with very few words. Roland's total lack of shame is the engine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Those shoes are a crime against humanity. They're orthopedic, they're beige, and they have the structural integrity of a marshmallow.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: They're comfortable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Comfortable? Patrick, they look like they were designed by someone who hates feet.

jokes.text: It's his brother, Brian. Although they could be friends too, I don't know, they could be brothers and friends. / Guys, how would you describe your relationship?
jokes.setup: Johnny says Roland needs to say goodbye to 'Carmine and his friend.'
jokes.punchline: Roland corrects 'friend' to 'brother Brian' but then immediately second-guesses himself and asks the guests to self-identify their relationship — in the middle of a serious conversation with Johnny.
jokes.explanation: Absurdist Roland moment — his tangent about the nature of Carmine and Brian's relationship, mid-confrontation, is a perfect character derailment. The 'Friendly?' response from the guests (implied) is a good button.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Did you just call me your boyfriend?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I literally just heard you say it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you misheard.

jokes.text: Okay, but for the sake of customer service, try to chill out, my man.
jokes.setup: Johnny demands Roland go weed the lawn immediately.
jokes.punchline: Roland advises Johnny to 'chill out' — invoking 'customer service' as a shield while he's the one who created the customer service problem.
jokes.explanation: Good Roland hypocrisy — using 'customer service' as cover for his own chaos. 'Chill out, my man' directed at Johnny is delightfully inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't say anything about boyfriends.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You said I was a bad boyfriend.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I said no such thing.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I'm embracing it. I'm a bad boyfriend. A bad, bad boyfriend.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, you're not a bad boyfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You literally just said I was.

jokes.text: Notice some of our more discreet backroom items are now on display at the front of the store.
jokes.setup: David returns from his errand to find plungers displayed at the front of the store.
jokes.punchline: David's formalized, passive-aggressive framing of 'more discreet backroom items' for toilet plungers — as if they are luxury items that require discretion — is peak David.
jokes.explanation: Excellent David voice — 'discreet backroom items' for plungers is both absurd and completely in character. His refusal to say 'plunger' is itself the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm taking off my shoes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, now you're just walking around in your socks like some kind of... sock enthusiast.

jokes.text: ...if assaulting customers with the sight of a toilet plunger the minute they walk in the door is something that you consider to be an effective business strategy, then that is a compromise that I am willing to make.
jokes.setup: Patrick presents the plunger display as David's opportunity to compromise.
jokes.punchline: David agrees to the 'compromise' while making it clear through his framing ('assaulting customers') that he finds it unconscionable — agreeing while poisoning the agreement.
jokes.explanation: 'Assaulting customers with the sight of a toilet plunger' is a beautifully dramatic phrase. David's reluctant agreement wrapped in condemnation is excellent character comedy — he's trying to compromise while being constitutionally incapable of not editorializing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was in the back, in what I can only describe as a VIP lounge. It was very exclusive, very intimate. Mahogany finishes, mood lighting, a certain... je ne sais quoi.

jokes.text: Are you sure? / Look at his face. / Look at your face.
jokes.setup: David appears to accept the plunger compromise.
jokes.punchline: Patrick and Stevie immediately clock that David's agreement is not genuine — 'look at his face' — and then David clocks their clocking — 'look at your face' — making the mutual surveillance funny.
jokes.explanation: Efficient three-way beat. The visual comedy of David's barely-contained disgust being immediately spotted is well-served by the staccato dialogue. Six words, maximum information about all three characters.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, this actually reminds me of the time your father met John Cougar Mellencamp at a benefit in the Hamptons. He spent the entire evening convinced it was John Cougar, not John Cougar Mellencamp, and kept correcting everyone. By the end of the night, John himself was confused about his own name.

jokes.text: Okay, great. Well, then obviously you'll have no problem if I get the toilet brushes out here, too.
jokes.setup: David has reluctantly agreed to the plunger display.
jokes.punchline: Patrick immediately escalates to toilet brushes — revealing this was always a gambit to expose David's limits. David's silent horror is the payoff.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.0 craft: Excellent escalation and structure. Patrick's move reveals he understood David perfectly and has been strategically deploying the compromise argument to push David further than he intended to go. The escalation from plungers to toilet brushes is calibrated exactly right — one step too far. The implied visual of David's face is the punchline, and it's earned by everything that came before.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, how long did it take you guys?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, it was years. Years and years.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Years?!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But you know what? It was worth the wait. He's my soulmate.

jokes.text: Is something wrong, David? / [long pause] / No.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if something is wrong after suggesting toilet brushes.
jokes.punchline: David's long pause followed by a clipped 'No' is a masterclass in repression — the pause IS the joke, and the denial is clearly false.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.1 craft: The scene cut from 11:15 to 11:19 to 11:24 — a full nine seconds — around a single 'No' is a comedic timing choice that relies entirely on Dan Levy's performance. The transcript captures it in the timestamp gaps. The pause is the joke; the 'No' is the button. Perfect economy (one syllable says everything), and the character integration is exceptional — only David would maintain this façade while visibly crumbling.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, you're way too drunk. I'm driving.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes! Finally, someone who understands me. You drive, darling. You drive us home in this... what is this, a Buick?

jokes.text: You do this every night you go out? / I do this every night.
jokes.setup: Alexis is watching Twyla's extensive pre-going-out routine.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's calm confirmation that yes, this elaborate ritual happens every night she goes out — with Twyla's warm deadpan — implies the effort-to-frequency ratio is very off for a small town bar.
jokes.explanation: A mild observational beat — the implication that Twyla has a very serious getting-ready ritual for Schitt's Creek's bar scene is funny. Not a big laugh but a warm character moment.

jokes.text: Oh you remind me of my days at the old Mudd Club on the Lower East Side. I remember one regrettably early morning with Johnny Thunders.
jokes.setup: Moira sees Alexis and Twyla getting ready to go out.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately pivots to name-dropping a deceased punk icon (Johnny Thunders died of a drug overdose) with 'regrettably early morning' — the casual implication of a wild night is vintage Moira.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira name-drop comedy — Johnny Thunders is a specific, deep-cut reference that implies Moira ran in genuinely wild circles. 'Regrettably early morning' as a euphemism is a great Moira circumlocution. High character integration.

jokes.text: Okay, can we help you with something? / Oh, I'm sorry, can't a mother show a little interest in her only daughter's social calendar?
jokes.setup: Moira has barged into the getting-ready session uninvited.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'can't a mother show a little interest' is funny because the entire first scene of the episode was about Moira demonstrating catastrophic disinterest in Alexis's emotional life.
jokes.explanation: Functional callback — Moira's claim to maternal interest is undermined by the cold open. The joke is the gap between her self-image and her actual behavior.

jokes.text: They don't allow you to drink and drive anymore.
jokes.setup: Moira asks how Alexis and Twyla are getting to the bar.
jokes.punchline: 'They don't allow you to drink and drive anymore' — the word 'anymore' implies this was previously allowed and Moira experienced that era.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.0 craft: The word 'anymore' is doing enormous work. Moira is not making a general statement about laws — she's implying personal historical experience with an era when DUI laws were more lax (or ignored). The line is one of the best examples in the episode of comedy through diction. Only Catherine O'Hara's Moira makes this land.

jokes.text: Oh dear, I have the night to myself, and I would like nothing better than to find a new man for Twyla.
jokes.setup: Moira announces she will drive them and wants to come along.
jokes.punchline: Moira's stated mission — 'find a new man for Twyla' — is a classic Moira redirect. She's framing her own desire to go out as an act of selfless matchmaking.
jokes.explanation: Good Moira character moment — she's clearly going for herself but has wrapped it in altruism. The specificity of 'a new man for Twyla' as her stated mission is funny.

jokes.text: I see before me, a beautiful young woman in her prime, who deserves every happiness. / Mrs. Rose, that's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me.
jokes.setup: Moira appears to be complimenting Twyla specifically.
jokes.punchline: The camera reveals (implied) Moira is looking past Twyla at herself in the mirror — but Twyla's genuine, moved response is the real punchline, making it bittersweet and funny simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.3 craft: A perfect Schitt's Creek joke — it's funny (Moira is talking to herself in the mirror) and warm (Twyla takes it as a genuine compliment and is deeply moved). The comedy and the heart are inseparable. The rewatch bonus is high because knowing the setup makes Twyla's line even funnier and sadder. Excellent structure — the visual misdirection is implied from context and lands hard.

jokes.text: Moira's compliment lands on Twyla: 'Mrs. Rose, that's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me.' — but Moira was talking about Twyla the whole time without knowing Twyla thought she meant herself
jokes.setup: Moira says 'I see before me, a beautiful young woman in her prime, who deserves every happiness' — appearing to be talking to Twyla
jokes.punchline: The implication is that Moira was describing Alexis (or her idea of the evening's purpose), but Twyla takes it as a personal compliment directed at her, creating a warm but slightly absurd disconnect
jokes.explanation: A warm-hearted misdirection that works on Twyla's sincere earnestness. Moira's 'And I mean every word of it' — which may or may not be directed at Twyla — adds an extra layer of ambiguity. The show uses Twyla's guileless warmth well here.

jokes.text: And I mean every word of it.
jokes.setup: Twyla is moved by Moira's 'compliment.'
jokes.punchline: Moira confirms 'I mean every word' — which she does, about herself. The irony is that she's being totally sincere, just misdirected.
jokes.explanation: A good button on the mirror joke. Moira's sincerity is technically accurate (she does mean it — about herself), which makes it a perfect irony.

jokes.text: Okay, I know what our car looks like, but thank you.
jokes.setup: Moira says the car is 'the black car out front.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'I know what our car looks like' is a mild but effective deflation of Moira's performative helpfulness.
jokes.explanation: Small beat. Gets a mild chuckle — Alexis's dry exasperation at Moira is well-rendered but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: If, and when, you meet someone who catches your eye, hold his gaze. Then walk up behind him, trace a single finger down his back. And if he follows you into a dark corner of the bar, it's meant to be.
jokes.setup: Moira offers dating advice from the car before they go in.
jokes.punchline: Moira's seduction technique — delivered with theatrical gravity — is both objectively predatory and hilariously specific. The solemnity of 'it's meant to be' applied to a bar corner encounter is absurd.
jokes.explanation: Moira's dating advice is funny because it's completely sincere and completely absurd. The step-by-step precision ('trace a single finger') delivered like ancient wisdom is very on-brand. Good character integration.

jokes.text: John Cougar... / No. / Mellencamp. / But guess who drove me home that night? / Mr. Rose. / Yes, you're right.
jokes.setup: Moira reveals her last target for the finger trick was John Cougar Mellencamp.
jokes.punchline: But it was Johnny Rose who drove her home — meaning Moira used her signature move on John Mellencamp, then ended up going home with her future husband instead. The romantic story is funny because it implies John Mellencamp didn't take the bait.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.0 craft: A wonderfully constructed story beat. The celebrity name-drop (John Cougar Mellencamp) is the fake-out; the real punch is that it was Johnny Rose who ended up taking her home, with the implication that Mellencamp rejected or ignored the move. 'Yes, you're right' as confirmation — rather than 'yes' — is a tiny piece of Moira grammar that's perfect. Alexis's 'No' as a preemptive refusal before the name is revealed is also a great beat.

jokes.text: And that car ride was better than any dark corner dalliance with JCM. / Ew.
jokes.setup: Moira has just told the story of how she met Johnny Rose.
jokes.punchline: Moira's romantic reflection on the car ride is undercut by Alexis's 'Ew' — which could refer to the JCM implication, or just to parents having romantic stories in general.
jokes.explanation: Good button. Alexis's 'Ew' is perfect Alexis — the warm romantic moment hits a wall of millennial disgust at parents' intimacy. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: Alexis! You're wrinkled in the back. / Ooh? What? / Confidence, girls!
jokes.setup: Alexis and Twyla are walking into the bar; Moira is calling from the car.
jokes.punchline: Moira's final words of wisdom — 'Confidence, girls!' — immediately follow pointing out a flaw, which is classically Moira: undermine and encourage in the same breath.
jokes.explanation: Lovely character moment — Moira's 'confidence' instruction following a critique of Alexis's wrinkle is perfectly Moira. The rhythm of 'Ooh? What?' from Alexis spinning around is a good visual beat inferred from context.

jokes.text: Roland playing video games in the motel office at 9 PM while Jocelyn is pregnant at home
jokes.setup: Johnny discovers Roland still at the motel long after work hours
jokes.punchline: Roland is playing video games — he's not doing extra work, he's hiding from his pregnant wife
jokes.explanation: The visual of Roland gaming in the motel office while ostensibly being a 'team player' is a solid sight gag that recontextualizes his earlier eagerness to be at the motel. His 'No, I'm not hiding out' while clearly hiding out is functional irony.

jokes.text: Here's the thing. I have just remembered how attentive Roland gets when I am pregnant. He is very hands on, very concerned, and he is just always... there.
jokes.setup: Johnny expects Jocelyn to be angry about Roland hiding at the motel.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's revelation — she's sending Roland to work late to get some alone time — is delivered with careful word choice: 'attentive,' 'hands on,' 'always there' are complaints dressed as compliments.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.0 craft: Jocelyn's speech is excellent because the comedy is entirely in the subtext. 'He is very hands on, very concerned, and he is just always... there' — each word is technically positive and actually devastating. The pause before 'there' is the key beat. This is a well-written piece of sustained deadpan that rewards attentive viewing. Strong character integration for Jocelyn.

jokes.text: Oh! / Wow! I just upgraded my wand! Two more levels and I'll have enough opals to buy the harp. / Way to go, honey!
jokes.setup: Johnny has just agreed to let Roland stay — Jocelyn has gotten what she wanted.
jokes.punchline: Roland's complete obliviousness — he's playing a fantasy game, excited about opals and a harp — while his wife has just orchestrated his banishment, and she cheers him on, is perfectly absurd.
jokes.explanation: Great button on the Jocelyn plot — Roland's excited wand upgrade, combined with Jocelyn's cheerful 'Way to go, honey!', makes her manipulation complete and sweet simultaneously. The specific details (opals, harp) are funnier than generic game references.

jokes.text: Alone time, Johnny. I just need some alone time.
jokes.setup: Roland celebrates his wand upgrade obliviously.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's direct address to Johnny — 'Alone time, Johnny. I just need some alone time.' — while Roland is cheerfully gaming inches away is a perfect piece of exasperated deadpan.
jokes.explanation: A good button on the Jocelyn revelation. The repetition of 'alone time' with the emphasis shift between the two sentences is excellent rhythm. Delivered while Roland is right there — he doesn't hear it, or doesn't care — is a great visual.

jokes.text: So, I don't think I'm gonna need a ride home tonight. / Oh my God, that's so great! / Because I think I'm going home with a guy.
jokes.setup: Twyla says she won't need a ride home — Alexis assumes something vague and positive.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's clarification is completely matter-of-fact: 'I'm going home with a guy' — delivered with Twyla's characteristic warmth and zero guile.
jokes.explanation: Sweet misdirection — Alexis's excitement precedes the clarification, and Twyla's guileless 'going home with a guy' is funny because of the contrast between her earnest delivery and the implication.

jokes.text: He plays soccer, and he works at the quarry, and we both have the same second favourite colour.
jokes.setup: Twyla explains why she's going home with this guy.
jokes.punchline: 'We both have the same second favourite colour' as a romantic compatibility point — the specificity of 'second favourite' is the absurdist kicker. Not favourite colour: second favourite.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.4 craft: 'The same second favourite colour' is a standout original joke. The escalation from soccer and quarry (normal) to 'second favourite colour' as the clinching compatibility metric is absurd, specific, and perfectly Twyla. The qualifier 'second' is everything — it implies both of them have given serious thought to their second favourite colour hierarchy. Highly quotable out of context, and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: So important.
jokes.setup: Twyla cites 'same second favourite colour' as a romantic selling point.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's dry 'So important' — which could be sincere or sarcastic, and is probably both — is a perfect reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: Two words that perfectly straddle sincerity and sarcasm. Alexis might actually find it important; she might be mocking it. The ambiguity is the joke.

jokes.text: Your mom's trick totally worked. I ran my finger down the backs of three different guys, and one of them turned around. It's a total numbers game.
jokes.setup: Twyla announces Moira's seduction technique worked.
jokes.punchline: Only one of three guys turned around — and Twyla characterizes this 33% success rate as a 'total numbers game' with complete optimism.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. The joke is that the technique barely worked (one of three), but Twyla's breezy 'total numbers game' reframes a mediocre result as evidence of a solid strategy. Strong character integration — only Twyla would find this encouraging. The callback adds significant value.

jokes.text: Hello? / Hey. / Hello? / Hey, can you hear me? / Just joking, you've got Ted's voicemail. Don't you hate it when people do that? Me too, and yet I just did it.
jokes.setup: Alexis calls Ted's number; someone appears to answer.
jokes.punchline: It's a voicemail greeting that mimics picking up — a meta joke about the annoying voicemail prank, followed by Ted's self-aware acknowledgment that he hates it and did it anyway.
jokes.explanation: Ted's voicemail is a perfectly constructed self-aware joke — the meta-commentary ('don't you hate it when people do that? Me too, and yet I just did it') is very much in Ted's cheerful, earnest character. The joke works on the call-in-the-dark emotional beat level too.

jokes.text: You know, I have to say, if we hadn't put these babies out there, there's a strong possibility we wouldn't have sold two of 'em today. / And a brush.
jokes.setup: Patrick makes a case that the plunger display was actually good for business.
jokes.punchline: David's resigned 'And a brush' — confirming that the toilet brushes also sold — is delivered as a concession that clearly costs him something.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful two-word callback payoff. 'And a brush' confirms Patrick's toilet brush gambit worked AND that David has been privately tracking this with horror. The economy is exceptional — the specificity of 'a brush' (singular, small consolation) versus two plungers is funny.

jokes.text: Fine, I'm terrible at compromise! There, I said it. Like Beyoncé, I excel as a solo artist, and I was also dressed by my mother well into my teens, okay?
jokes.setup: David's resistance to the plungers has collapsed into a full confession.
jokes.punchline: The Beyoncé comparison as an explanation for being bad at compromise — and the strange appendage 'dressed by my mother well into my teens' as parallel evidence — is a brilliantly weird David Rose construction.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.7 craft / 9.0 impact: This is the episode's standout comedic moment. The Beyoncé comparison is already unexpected and funny; the pivot to 'I was also dressed by my mother well into my teens' is bizarre, non-sequitur, and somehow perfectly logical coming from David. It reveals backstory, character, and insecurity in two sentences while being genuinely hilarious. The 'okay?' at the end, seeking validation, is the perfect button. This is the line that makes the episode memorable. High quotability — 'Like Beyoncé, I excel as a solo artist' has standalone life outside the episode.

jokes.text: These mountaineering shoes that my boyfriend is wearing, looking like Oprah on a Thanksgiving Day hike, incorrect.
jokes.setup: David is cataloguing things he finds 'incorrect' — plungers, breath mints — and escalates to Patrick's shoes.
jokes.punchline: 'Looking like Oprah on a Thanksgiving Day hike' is a shockingly specific image. It's an insult with a complete visual, and it accidentally reveals that David thinks about Patrick's appearance in elaborate celebrity-comparison terms.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.6 craft / 9.0 impact: 'Looking like Oprah on a Thanksgiving Day hike' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. The joke is: (1) the image is very specific and complete, (2) it's delivered in the middle of a rant about plungers and breath mints, making the pivot to boyfriend's shoes unexpected, (3) the qualifier 'Thanksgiving Day' is what elevates it from good to great — why Thanksgiving specifically? — and (4) it accidentally reveals David's deep investment in Patrick's aesthetic. Extremely high quotability. This and the Beyoncé line are the two most memorable of the episode.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, what did you just say? / I said the breath mints need to move. / I think it was something about your boyfriend's shoes?
jokes.setup: David has accidentally called Patrick his 'boyfriend' in the middle of a rant.
jokes.punchline: David immediately backtracks to 'I said the breath mints need to move' — Stevie kindly/mischievously clarifies what was actually said.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.0 craft: A perfect relationship-comedy beat. David accidentally reveals his true feelings by calling Patrick his 'boyfriend' mid-rant about plungers. His immediate panic backtrack and Stevie's gentle assistance ('I think it was something about your boyfriend's shoes?') is perfectly executed. The subtext — David and Patrick's undefined relationship — gives this enormous weight beyond the surface joke. Strong character integration for all three.

jokes.text: I mean, or not. I don't remember saying it. So, you can do whatever you'd like.
jokes.setup: Patrick offers to take off his shoes since his 'boyfriend' doesn't like them.
jokes.punchline: David's desperate backtrack — 'I don't remember saying it. So, you can do whatever you'd like' — is the comedy of someone trying to un-ring a bell.
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the bit. David's 'I don't remember saying it' after three characters confirmed it is classic David self-protective denial. The sentence rhythm of his backtrack is well-written.

jokes.text: I think my work here is done. / And what work is that, exactly?
jokes.setup: Stevie has just assisted in exposing David's 'boyfriend' slip.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's satisfied 'I think my work here is done' implies she engineered this — Patrick's 'And what work is that, exactly?' punctures her satisfaction while confirming she's been playing chess.
jokes.explanation: Good button on the Patrick/David/Stevie scene. Stevie's satisfaction and Patrick's gentle questioning of it reveals their dynamic — Stevie has been enjoying this entire situation.

jokes.text: My boyfriend doesn't like the shoes, so I'm gonna take the shoes off. / Okay, before you do that, I just wanna let you know that sock feet in a public place is also incorrect. / We do what we have to do.
jokes.setup: Patrick moves to take off his shoes — the compromise David stumbled into.
jokes.punchline: David can't let the shoe removal happen without noting that sock feet are also wrong — 'incorrect' — and Patrick's serene 'We do what we have to do' is the perfect unbothered button.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — David has now created a situation where both the shoes and the lack of shoes are 'incorrect.' Patrick's zen acceptance of the impossible situation is very Patrick. The callback to David's 'incorrect' usage is a nice structural echo.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'We do what we have to do.' — as he removes his shoes in a public retail space
jokes.setup: David has declared sock feet in public also incorrect
jokes.punchline: Patrick's philosophical acceptance — 'We do what we have to do' — as he proceeds to go sock-footed in their store is an excellent button
jokes.explanation: Patrick's Zen acceptance of an absurd situation is very in character. He's discovered that the path of least resistance with David is to simply proceed. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Hey. You were in the bar? How did I not see you? / I... I wanted to wait for you, but I got bored in the car.
jokes.setup: Alexis exits the bar alone to find Moira outside — but Moira has been inside the whole time.
jokes.punchline: Moira's admission — she 'wanted to wait' but got bored — confirms she was inside the bar the entire time, completely violating her stated role as supportive chauffeur.
jokes.explanation: Good Moira beat — 'I got bored in the car' is completely Moira. She lasted approximately zero minutes as the self-sacrificing chauffeur. The casual confession is the joke.

jokes.text: Bored, so I found a lovely little speakeasy at the back of the club.
jokes.setup: Moira explains what she did instead of waiting in the car.
jokes.punchline: Moira has found and settled into a 'speakeasy' at the back of a small-town bar — 'speakeasy' being a very Moira word for what is probably just a back room. Her comfort level with finding hidden bars is revealing.
jokes.explanation: 'Lovely little speakeasy' is a great Moira phrase — it glamorizes a presumably unglamorous back room of a small-town bar. Her instinct to immediately find the most exclusive area available is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Well, I... I didn't want to say this in front of Twyla but... there was a reason I drew Mr. Cougar Mellencamp into that dark corner of the bar instead of your father. / I don't need to know how this story ends, so...
jokes.setup: Moira begins to share a confidence about why she targeted John Cougar Mellencamp.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's pre-emptive 'I don't need to know how this story ends' — delivered before Moira finishes — is the correct response to a parent beginning a story about their romantic/sexual choices.
jokes.explanation: Good callback continuation. Alexis's defensive cut-off is the right child reaction. 'Mr. Cougar Mellencamp' as a form of address is a funny Moira choice — formal title for a rock star.

jokes.text: there was a reason I drew Mr. Cougar Mellencamp into that dark corner of the bar instead of your father. / Your father was with someone at the time.
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining why she targeted Mellencamp first.
jokes.punchline: John Rose was with someone else when Moira deployed her move — 'your father was with someone at the time' — which recontextualizes the origin story: Moira was originally after someone else and pivoted to Johnny.
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8.1 craft: This is a dark and funny piece of lore — the Roses' love story began because Moira wanted someone else and Johnny was unavailable. The detail 'your father was with someone at the time' is a throwaway that implies an entire other chapter of backstory. Delivered to Alexis, who just wants her mother to stop talking, it lands as both funny and subtly moving.

jokes.text: Well, that's very cute for you, but I don't know what that has to do with me.
jokes.setup: Moira delivers a heartfelt point about love being worth waiting for.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's defensive dismissal — 'that's very cute for you' — completely deflects Moira's obvious parallel while the audience understands it perfectly.
jokes.explanation: 'That's very cute for you' is a signature Alexis phrase — simultaneously dismissive and weirdly complimentary. The audience's dramatic irony (we see the parallel Alexis denies) makes the laugh slightly bittersweet.

jokes.text: I'm driving? / That's a good idea!
jokes.setup: They're heading to the car after the bar.
jokes.punchline: Alexis discovers (or realizes) she's driving despite Moira offering to be the 'chauffeur' — Moira's enthusiastic 'That's a good idea!' confirms she's been drinking and has no intention of driving.
jokes.explanation: Good callback button to close the Moira chauffeur plot. Her 'That's a good idea!' confirms she's been drinking and has gently outsourced the driving back to Alexis. The callback to her offering to drive — and to the 'they don't allow you to drink and drive anymore' line — makes this land harder for attentive viewers.

tmdb_overview: Alexis and Twyla have a girls' night out and Patrick teaches David about compromise.
insight: David and Patrick's absence lets character-comedy shine; 49 jokes land mostly through understatement.
